sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
The RIGHT key changed 8 cells in the bbox [40, 10, 41, 13]. This is around the green center of the gray box at the bottom. Let me check what changed there.
The green center might have moved or changed. Let me inspect the current state to understand what the directional keys do.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check what changed with RIGHT key
prev_seg = previous_frame.segmentation
curr_seg = current_frame.segmentation

# Compare the green box area
lines = current_frame.ascii.split('\n')
print("=== Bottom area (green box) ===")
for r in range(38, 45):
    row_str = lines[r][6:20]
    print(f"Row {r}: {row_str}")

# Check segmentation for changes in that area
prev_nodes = {n['id']: n for n in prev_seg['nodes']}
curr_nodes = {n['id']: n for n in curr_seg['nodes']}

print("\n=== Changes in bottom area ===")
for nid in sorted(curr_nodes.keys()):
    cn = curr_nodes[nid]
    pn = prev_nodes.get(nid)
    r0, c0 = cn['boundary'][0]
    if 35 <= r0 <= 45 and c0 < 20:
        if pn is None or pn['hash'] != cn['hash'] or pn['color'] != cn['color'] or pn['pixels'] != cn['pixels']:
            print(f"  Node {nid}: color={cn['color']}, px={cn['pixels']}, pos=({r0},{c0})")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

The green center (N) moved from position (40, 10) to (40, 8) - it shifted left by 2 columns. Wait, actually looking at the ascii:
- Previous: Row 40 had "ccggNNgg" at cols 8-15 (green at cols 10-11)
- Current: Row 40 has "ccggggNN" at cols 8-15 (green at cols 12-13)
Wait, let me re-read. The current output shows:
Row 40: ccggggNNcccccc
So the green center moved from cols 10-11 to cols 12-13. That's a shift to the RIGHT by 2 columns. This makes sense - I pressed RIGHT and the green center moved right.
So the directional keys move the colored center inside the gray box! The gray box acts as a container, and the colored center can move within it.
This changes my understanding of the game. The gray boxes with colored centers are like "cursors" or "selectors" that can be moved with directional keys. The colored center represents the current selection.
Let me explore this more. There are two gray boxes with colored centers:
1. Top: gray box with yellow center
2. Bottom: gray box with green center

    print(f"Row {r}: {row_str}")
